title: Kourabiedes
subtitle: Greek almond shortbread with a thick icing sugar coating
excerpt: The traditional Greek Christmas almond shortbread: buttery, crumbly, and buried under so much icing sugar you cannot tell the shape. Brandy in the dough gives warmth and depth.
sourceNotes: Kourabiedes (also spelled kourabiethes) are the defining Greek festive shortbread, baked throughout Greece and Cyprus at Christmas and for weddings and baptisms. The clove pressed into each biscuit symbolically represents the clove brought by the Magi. They are closely related to Turkish kurabiye and the wider Eastern Mediterranean shortbread family. The recipe appears in Diane Kochilas's The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001) and Tessa Kiros's Food from Many Greek Kitchens (2011).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and coated kourabiedes in a single layer, then bag. Re-dust with icing sugar after thawing.
recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely; the dough is easy to double.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baked kourabiedes keep in an airtight tin for 2 to 3 weeks. They often taste better after a few days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; pale golden is the target — no browning
baking.decoratingTechnique: buried in a deep layer of icing sugar immediately after baking

content.text: The biscuits should not brown. Pale, barely golden edges are the maximum. At 160°C fan they need about 18 to 20 minutes, but individual ovens vary significantly. Kourabiedes are delicate when hot and firm as they cool; do not try to move them until they have had at least 5 minutes on the tray.

Use unsalted butter beaten at room temperature until very pale and fluffy before adding anything else. The extended beating incorporates air that gives the shortbread its characteristic light, crumbly texture. Cutting this step short produces a denser biscuit.
Make the dough and chill, 30 minutes

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} with a hand mixer on high speed for 5 minutes until very pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{icing-sugar}}, then the {{vanilla-extract}} and {{brandy}}. Fold in the ground {{almonds}}, then the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}}. The dough will be soft. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until firm enough to shape.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Shape the chilled dough into rounds or crescents of about 20 g each. Place on the trays, spaced 3 cm apart. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until just barely pale golden at the edges with no browning on top. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes.

Sift a generous layer of coating {{icing-sugar}} onto a tray or board. While the biscuits are still warm, carefully transfer them to the icing sugar and sift more over the top to bury them completely. Leave to cool in the icing sugar for 15 minutes. Lift out carefully, leaving a thick coating on each biscuit. Store in an airtight tin between sheets of baking paper.
